      "content": "# Schemas\r\n\r\nThis directory contains the machine-readable registry and validation artifacts for XanaNode.\r\n\r\nThe current layout is versioned and split between canonical type registries and JSON Schemas that validate substrate metadata and reports.\r\n\r\nCore files:\r\n\r\n- [xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json](xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json)\r\n- [xananode-node-types.schema.v0.3.0.json](xananode-node-types.schema.v0.3.0.json)\r\n- [xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json](xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json)\n- [xananode-relationship-types.schema.v0.5.0.json](xananode-relationship-types.schema.v0.5.0.json)\n- [substrate-manifest.schema.json](substrate-manifest.schema.json)\n- [substrate-node.schema.json](substrate-node.schema.json)\n- [substrate-relationships.schema.json](substrate-relationships.schema.json)\n- [property-registry.schema.json](property-registry.schema.json)\n- [xananode-property-registry.v0.1.0.json](xananode-property-registry.v0.1.0.json)\n- [substrate-layout.schema.json](substrate-layout.schema.json)\n- [xananode-substrate-layout.v0.1.0.json](xananode-substrate-layout.v0.1.0.json)\n- [namespace.schema.json](namespace.schema.json)\n- [merge-report.schema.json](merge-report.schema.json)\n- [compatibility-report.schema.json](compatibility-report.schema.json)\n- [substrate-diff.schema.json](substrate-diff.schema.json)\n- [author-profile.schema.json](author-profile.schema.json)\n- [nanopublication.schema.json](nanopublication.schema.json)\n- [ro-crate-metadata.schema.json](ro-crate-metadata.schema.json)\n\r\nThe versioned registry files define the canonical core node, relationship, property, and substrate layout conventions. The schema files describe the required structure of each registry plus related substrate and compatibility documents.\n\nSchemas and schema registries are licensed under `Apache-2.0` so implementations can adopt them freely while preserving attribution and provenance.\n",

      "content": "# Compatibility Levels\n\nCompatibility levels describe how safely one implementation can ingest or federate another substrate.\n\n## Level 0: Opaque\n\nThe substrate can be stored as raw data, but its schema is unknown.\n\n## Level 1: Manifest Recognized\n\nThe substrate declares a manifest and namespace.\n\n## Level 2: Core Valid\n\nThe substrate validates against XanaNode Core.\n\n## Level 3: Extensions Declared\n\nThe substrate uses custom types, but those types are declared in namespaced extension schemas.\n\n## Level 4: Merge Candidate\n\nThe substrate can participate in identity matching, schema mapping, and merge reporting.\n\n## Level 5: Federation Ready\n\nThe substrate supports stable IDs, provenance, declared schemas, merge reports, and compatibility reports.\n\nCompatibility is not a moral ranking. It is a practical statement about how much automated interoperation is safe.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/compatibility-levels.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:9e8e8ee8beaf1a7d1f3fc872d4c60dc17a7c7713c737009b85da5a3bdd38cc29",

      "artifact_path": "specs/federation.md",

      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Federation\n\nFederation is the process of connecting independently authored XanaNode substrates.\n\nFederation does not require one canonical truth source.\n\nInstead, it preserves local substrates and creates mappings among them.\n\n## Federation Inputs\n\n- substrate manifests\n- node registries\n- relationship registries\n- custom extension schemas\n- provenance metadata\n- merge reports\n\n## Federation Outputs\n\n- compatibility reports\n- identity mappings\n- schema mappings\n- conflict reports\n- combined analysis views\n\n## Core Rule\n\nNever merge by destroying context.\n\nMerge by adding structure.\n\n## Pack Composition\n\nFederation, local pack ingress, and substrate merging share one protocol model. A pack is a bounded substrate or substrate fragment that can be composed with another substrate in one of three modes:\n\n- `mounted`: keep the external governance boundary. The receiving substrate may render, traverse, cite, and analyze the pack without copying it into canonical local authorship.\n- `imported`: copy pack records into generated local artifacts while preserving provenance and pack metadata. Import does not imply duplicate collapse or identity reconciliation.\n- `merged`: reconcile the incoming pack with local records through explicit identity mappings, merge candidates, conflict reports, and review policy.\n\nMounting is reversible and is the default for optional packs. Importing is an auditable intake step. Merging is a stronger federation operation and must preserve disagreement, provenance, and local context instead of overwriting them.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/federation.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:a197a48cf7aba6a0a0cc1a75587edfedeca59676283ee3039710eaddc23bbd6f",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/federation.md",
        "method": "archive",

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-implementation-compatibility.md",
      "title": "Specs / Implementation Compatibility",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "specs/implementation-compatibility.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/implementation-compatibility.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/implementation-compatibility.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Implementation Compatibility\n\nAn implementation may describe itself as XanaNode-compatible when it validates, emits, imports, or projects XanaNode protocol artifacts according to the canonical specification and registries.\n\nImplementations should identify themselves clearly and reference the canonical specification:\n\n```text\nThis project is a XanaNode-compatible implementation.\nCanonical specification: https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode\n```\n\nIf the implementation supports only part of the protocol, it should say so:\n\n```text\nThis project is a partial XanaNode-compatible renderer for mounted substrate packs.\n```\n\nImplementations should not imply they are the canonical XanaNode project unless they are maintained as part of the canonical project. Compatibility means interoperability, not ownership.\n\nRecommended metadata:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"implements\": \"xananode-protocol\",\n  \"compatibility\": \"xananode-compatible\",\n  \"specification\": \"https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode\",\n  \"supported_artifacts\": [\"substrate.json\", \"nodes.json\", \"relationships.json\"]\n}\n```\n\nThis convention supports legal attribution, community attribution, brand recognition, and independent federation.\n\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/implementation-compatibility.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:ae0a610b3ab7207f0daa026baa104b1b95aaf85b96634b0fdf44a738a6b7de09",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/implementation-compatibility.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:ae0a610b3ab7207f0daa026baa104b1b95aaf85b96634b0fdf44a738a6b7de09",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-interoperability.md",
      "title": "Specs / Interoperability",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "specs/interoperability.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/interoperability.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/interoperability.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Interoperability Profiles\n\nXanaNode keeps its author-facing JSON simple, but it should map cleanly to established standards.\n\n## JSON-LD\n\nThe canonical JSON-LD context lives at [../contexts/xananode.context.jsonld](../contexts/xananode.context.jsonld).\n\nImplementations should use the context when exporting nodes, relationships, fragments, and substrate packages into Linked Data environments.\n\n## Web Annotation Selectors\n\nFragment selectors should use Web Annotation selector names where possible:\n\n- `TextQuoteSelector`\n- `TextPositionSelector`\n- `FragmentSelector`\n- `CssSelector`\n- `XPathSelector`\n- `RangeSelector`\n\nThe older `semantic-anchor` selector remains acceptable for human-authored stable anchors.\n\n## PROV-O\n\nXanaNode provenance fields map to PROV-O concepts:\n\n- nodes and relationship assertions are `prov:Entity`\n- people, organizations, projects, and external actors are `prov:Agent`\n- creation, assertion, review, import, and publication are `prov:Activity`\n- `created_by` maps to `prov:wasAttributedTo`\n- `created_at` maps to `prov:generatedAtTime`\n- `derived_from` maps to derivation provenance\n\n## Content IDs\n\nHuman-readable protocol IDs remain the stable author-facing identifiers. Implementations may add immutable content identifiers:\n\n- `protocol_id`: stable XanaNode ID\n- `content_id`: immutable hash or CID-like identity for the content\n- `version_id`: version identity such as a Git commit/path reference\n\nContent IDs should not replace protocol IDs. They support verification, fixity, and preservation.\n\n## Preservation Packages\n\nSubstrates may include RO-Crate-compatible companion metadata named `ro-crate-metadata.json`. This metadata should describe the substrate package, schema references, rights, authorship, fixity, and dependencies.\n\n## Nanopublication Profile\n\nHigh-value claim nodes may be exported in a nanopublication-like package separating:\n\n- assertion\n- provenance\n- publication information\n\nThis is optional but useful for scholarly, legal, scientific, and archival substrates.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/interoperability.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:ee9ccb3e98730c558dff0e2adc272bce9055430542e634e77524dfef8fd79a1e",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/interoperability.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:ee9ccb3e98730c558dff0e2adc272bce9055430542e634e77524dfef8fd79a1e",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-merge-validation.md",
      "title": "Specs / Merge Validation",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "specs/merge-validation.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/merge-validation.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/merge-validation.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Merge Validation\n\nMerge validation checks whether multiple substrates can be analyzed together.\n\nIt should verify:\n\n- each substrate has a namespace\n- each node has a stable ID\n- relationship types are declared\n- custom types are declared\n- required fields exist\n- target nodes can be resolved or marked external\n- conflicts are reported rather than silently overwritten\n\nA merge report is not a final truth statement. It is a record of what the merge process found.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/merge-validation.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:bc771268de15b40321daa2a8c7f5432a7882d31f2931b59403c48e14a8636676",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/merge-validation.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:bc771268de15b40321daa2a8c7f5432a7882d31f2931b59403c48e14a8636676",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-namespaces.md",
      "title": "Specs / Namespaces",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "specs/namespaces.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/namespaces.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/namespaces.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Namespaces\n\nNamespaces prevent independently authored substrates from colliding.\n\nA namespace identifies who owns a local vocabulary or substrate.\n\nExamples:\n\n```text\nxananode:claim\nexample.museum:artifact\nexample.researcher_a:concept/knowledge-substrate\n```\n\nThe core namespace is `xananode`.\n\nCustom substrates should use their own namespace.\n\nA namespace should be declared in a substrate manifest and in any extension schema files.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/namespaces.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:ba8fec7ddf921b1f75b58104e07b8710f97a77bc53d40cdc1c2c99a030d18beb",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/namespaces.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:ba8fec7ddf921b1f75b58104e07b8710f97a77bc53d40cdc1c2c99a030d18beb",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-nodes.md",
      "title": "Specs / Nodes",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "specs/nodes.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/nodes.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/nodes.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Nodes\r\n\r\nNodes are addressable knowledge objects.\r\n\r\nA node may represent a person, concept, claim, question, hypothesis, problem, knowledge gap, communication, response, source, observation, essay, media item, event, place, organization, project, technology, publication, community, relationship, revision, trail, schema, or fragment.\n\r\nNodes should have stable IDs and human-readable summaries.\r\n\r\nNodes should carry provenance metadata when it is known. Common provenance fields include `created_by`, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `source_url`, `rights_status`, and `confidence`.\r\n\r\n`created_by` should point to a person, organization, project, or external actor identifier when possible. If the actor is local to the substrate, it should be represented as a node.\r\n\r\nA node is not automatically true. It is an object in the substrate that can be connected to evidence, claims, sources, disputes, and revisions.\n\n## Inquiry And Knowledge States\n\nKnowledge work is not only a collection of asserted facts. It also contains questions, provisional explanations, missing evidence, and unresolved problems. XanaNode models these as first-class nodes so a substrate can show what it knows, what it does not know, and what it is actively trying to find out.\n\nUse `question` for an explicit inquiry or prompt. A question may be answered, partially answered, raised by a source, asked in a communication, or connected to a knowledge gap.\n\nUse `hypothesis` for a provisional explanation or prediction. A hypothesis should be connected to tests, evidence, counterevidence, validation, falsification, and revision history.\n\nUse `problem` for a difficulty, risk, design challenge, or research problem that organizes work.\n\nUse `knowledge_gap` for a known absence: missing citation, unknown author, lost document, incomplete context, unresolved identity, or evidence that has not been found yet.\n\nCommon epistemic workflow properties include `uncertainty_level`, `review_status`, `research_priority`, and `evidence_strength`. These fields are also listed in the property registry so tools can parse them consistently.\n\nExample:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"id\": \"example.minimal:question/why-relationships-matter\",\n  \"title\": \"Why do relationships matter?\",\n  \"type\": \"question\",\n  \"status\": \"open\",\n  \"importance\": 5,\n  \"summary\": \"An inquiry into why preserving relationship structure changes what a knowledge system can do.\",\n  \"uncertainty_level\": \"medium\",\n  \"review_status\": \"needs_review\",\n  \"research_priority\": 4,\n  \"relationships\": []\n}\n```\n\n## Communication Nodes\n\nCommunication should not always be flattened into a simple edge such as `authored` or `discusses`. A communication can have topology, participants, directionality, feedback, medium, audience, and consequences. When those properties matter, model the communication itself as a `communication` node.\n\nUse `communication_model` to describe participant topology:\n\n- `one_to_one`\n- `one_to_many`\n- `many_to_one`\n- `many_to_many`\n\nUse `communication_pattern` to describe the semantic pattern:\n\n- `dialogue`\n- `broadcast`\n- `collection`\n- `coordination`\n- `negotiation`\n- `debate`\n- `consensus`\n- `instruction`\n- `query_response`\n- `publication`\n\nUse `response` for an answer, reply, rebuttal, clarification, or follow-up. Responses can `answers` a question, `replied_to` another response or communication, or be `answered_in` a communication context.\n\n## Primary Type And Facets\n\nEach node has one primary `type`. The primary type controls the node's stable route, required fields, default rendering, and validation behavior.\n\nNodes may also carry a narrower `subtype`. The subtype does not replace the primary type. It describes a more specific kind inside that route and validation category. For example, a node can remain a `source` while declaring `subtype: \"git_repository\"`, `subtype: \"official_site\"`, `subtype: \"documentation\"`, `subtype: \"support_page\"`, or `subtype: \"web_snapshot\"`.\n\nUse `subtypes` when one node needs multiple narrow labels. Subtypes should use lower-case slugs. Namespaced subtypes are allowed for extension vocabularies.\n\nSome real knowledge objects naturally play several roles. A quotation can be a `fragment` of a source, evidence used like a `source`, and an assertion that functions like a `claim`. Do not duplicate the quotation into separate nodes just to satisfy those categories. Model one canonical node and add secondary `facets` when the object should participate in more than one role.\n\nExample:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"id\": \"example.minimal:fragment/as-we-may-think-0004\",\n  \"title\": \"Associative Trails Quote\",\n  \"type\": \"fragment\",\n  \"facets\": [\"source\", \"claim\"],\n  \"summary\": \"A stable quotation fragment that can be cited as evidence and interpreted as an assertion.\"\n}\n```\n\nUse relationships to explain why a facet matters in context. For example, a quote can `supports` a claim, `derived_from` a source, or `defines` a concept. Facets describe secondary behavior; relationships describe meaning.\n\nProjection layers should show those secondary roles instead of hiding them. The primary `type` keeps authority over routing, validation, and default presentation, but a graph projection should visually mix the primary type color with any facet colors it recognizes. In other words, a quote that is primarily a `fragment` and also has `source` and `claim` facets should not look like only a fragment. Its visual mark should intertwingle the fragment, source, and claim colors by slices, bands, rings, or another accessible mixed-color treatment.\n\nIf a projection cannot use color, it should preserve the distinction with labels, texture, stroke patterns, badges, or another cue. A renderer may simplify the treatment at tiny sizes, but it should not erase the fact that the node carries multiple roles while claiming full projection compliance.\n\n## Media Assets\n\nMedia should be represented as `media` nodes, then connected to other nodes with `has_primary_media`, `depicts`, `represents`, `transcribes`, or another appropriate relationship. A node that wants a representative image should use `primary_media` to reference a media node rather than embedding a renderer-specific image path.\n\nPortable media nodes should use a relative `asset_path` when the file is part of the substrate or pack:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"id\": \"example.minimal:media/hugo-site-snapshot\",\n  \"title\": \"Hugo Site Snapshot\",\n  \"type\": \"media\",\n  \"subtype\": \"web_snapshot\",\n  \"media_type\": \"screenshot\",\n  \"mime_type\": \"image/png\",\n  \"asset_path\": \"assets/sources/hugo-official-site/snapshot.png\",\n  \"asset_role\": \"source_snapshot\",\n  \"source_url\": \"https://gohugo.io/\",\n  \"source_snapshot\": {\n    \"captured_at\": \"2026-06-19T00:00:00Z\",\n    \"source_url\": \"https://gohugo.io/\",\n    \"method\": \"screenshot\",\n    \"tool\": \"xananode-core\"\n  },\n  \"rights_status\": \"external\",\n  \"importance\": 3,\n  \"summary\": \"A captured visual representation of the Hugo official site.\",\n  \"relationships\": []\n}\n```\n\n`asset_path` is always relative to the substrate or pack root. It must not escape that root. Implementations that build packs must copy referenced assets into the pack and preserve the relative path, or rewrite the path and record that rewrite in pack metadata.\n\nMedia nodes should include stable content identifiers, usually `content_id: \"sha256:...\"`, whenever a tool can compute them. Content identifiers let federation tools detect duplicate files, distinguish duplicate bytes from duplicate titles, and explain how a file moved through different substrates.\n\nRecommended asset layout:\n\n```text\nassets/\n  media/\n  projection/\n    node-types/\n      <type>.svg\n    node-subtypes/\n      <type>/\n        <subtype>.svg\n  sources/\n    <source-node-local-id>/\n      snapshot.png\n      thumbnail.png\n      metadata.json\n```\n\nSource page previews, Open Graph images, screenshots, transcripts, and archived copies should be captured as media nodes with provenance. The external `source_url` remains the live source. The media node records the captured representation and its rights status.\n\n## Type And Subtype Projection Media\n\nNode type registries may define projection media metadata under each node type's `projection` object. This lets projection layers draw a stable visual mark for a type without inventing renderer-local mappings.\n\nThe canonical fields are:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"type\": \"concept\",\n  \"projection\": {\n    \"icon\": \"concept\",\n    \"icon_label\": \"C\",\n    \"asset_path\": \"assets/projection/node-types/concept.svg\",\n    \"subtype_asset_path_template\": \"assets/projection/node-subtypes/concept/{subtype}.svg\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nWhen a substrate or pack includes the actual icon file, it should also include a `media` node for that file. The media node should use `asset_role: \"node_type_projection_icon\"` or `asset_role: \"node_subtype_projection_icon\"`, carry `asset_path`, `media_type`, `mime_type`, `alt`, `rights_status`, and provenance, and connect to the schema/type node when that type is represented as a node.\n\nOfficial XanaNode projections use this order for a node's center mark:\n\n1. primary media when the node has an explicit `primary_media` or resolved visual media\n2. subtype projection media when a subtype-specific asset is available\n3. node type projection media\n4. registry `icon` or `icon_label` fallback\n\nThe node title remains the title and should be shown outside the center mark. Type and subtype labels may be shown as subtle chips, legends, tooltips, or inspector metadata. They should not replace the title.\n\n## Branding As Knowledge\n\nBranding is part of substrate identity and provenance. A logo, icon, color palette, tagline, or author mark should not live only inside renderer configuration. If a project, organization, author, or substrate changes its branding, that change has context and should be traceable.\n\nRepresent brand assets as `media` nodes, usually with `subtype: \"logo\"` or `subtype: \"image\"`. Reference those media nodes from substrate manifests or author profiles through their `branding` blocks:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"branding\": {\n    \"brand_name\": \"XanaNode\",\n    \"tagline\": \"Relationships preserve knowledge\",\n    \"icon_media\": \"example.minimal:media/xananode-icon\",\n    \"primary_color\": \"#55D6BE\",\n    \"accent_color\": \"#FF8C00\",\n    \"reason\": \"Protocol branding is modeled as substrate metadata and media.\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nRenderers should treat branding metadata as a projection input, not as the canonical source. The canonical source is the profile or manifest plus the referenced media node and its asset/provenance fields.\n\n## Software And Build Metadata\n\nSoftware projects, SDKs, renderers, workspace layers, desktop applications, and generated packs should carry version and build provenance when known.\n\nUse `software_version` for the package or release version. Use `build_metadata` for the exact build context:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"software_version\": \"0.1.0\",\n  \"build_metadata\": {\n    \"git_commit\": \"abc123\",\n    \"git_branch\": \"main\",\n    \"built_at\": \"2026-06-19T00:00:00Z\",\n    \"built_by\": \"@xananode/core\",\n    \"runtime\": \"node\",\n    \"dependencies\": [\n      {\n        \"name\": \"@xananode/core\",\n        \"version\": \"0.1.0\",\n        \"relationship\": \"uses\"\n      }\n    ]\n  }\n}\n```\n\nCompiled applications should record the version and compile/build date in their own metadata and, when represented in a substrate, on the project or technology node that describes that application. Generated canonical packs should include enough build metadata to identify which protocol, core, renderer, and workspace versions produced them.\n\n## Open Properties\n\nSubstrate nodes may carry extra root-level properties so authors can describe domain-specific facts without waiting for a protocol release. Common extra properties should use the canonical property registry when possible.\n\nThe core property registry standardizes fields such as `birth_date`, `geo_coordinates`, `currency_value`, `measurement_si`, and `external_identifier`. Implementations can use `schemas/xananode-property-registry.v0.1.0.json` to parse these values consistently while still accepting custom extension properties.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/nodes.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:258b7a00b5e128d3db5ab717a20581e9f8cefcdc4b09543ae136cf87d4e2e21e",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/nodes.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:258b7a00b5e128d3db5ab717a20581e9f8cefcdc4b09543ae136cf87d4e2e21e",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }

    {

      "content": "# Specs\r\n\r\nThis directory contains the normative protocol definitions for XanaNode.\r\n\r\nThe spec set should be read alongside the versioned registries in [schemas/](../schemas), especially the core node and relationship registries.\r\n\r\nKey documents:\r\n\r\n- [xananode-core.md](xananode-core.md)\r\n- [substrates.md](substrates.md)\r\n- [nodes.md](nodes.md)\r\n- [relationships.md](relationships.md)\r\n- [namespaces.md](namespaces.md)\r\n- [federation.md](federation.md)\r\n- [merge-validation.md](merge-validation.md)\r\n- [transclusion.md](transclusion.md)\r\n- [tumbler-addressing.md](tumbler-addressing.md)\n- [compatibility-levels.md](compatibility-levels.md)\n- [interoperability.md](interoperability.md)\n- [studio.md](studio.md)\n- [implementation-compatibility.md](implementation-compatibility.md)\n\nThe current core registry files are versioned and define the protocol vocabulary used throughout the repository.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/README.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:a7d0d63d0cd4d091260eec44895c930d06278ba1dbcbaf0816770033ed2bea28",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/README.md",

        "content_id": "sha256:a7d0d63d0cd4d091260eec44895c930d06278ba1dbcbaf0816770033ed2bea28",

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-relationships.md",
      "title": "Specs / Relationships",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "specs/relationships.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/relationships.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/relationships.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Relationships\r\n\r\nRelationships connect nodes with explicit meaning.\r\n\r\nXanaNode relationships are typed.\r\n\r\nExamples:\n\n- `supports`\n- `contradicts`\n- `explains`\n- `answers`\n- `investigates`\n- `requires_source`\n- `possibly_related_to`\n- `created`\n- `derived_from`\n- `communicated_to`\n- `transcludes`\n- `deep_links_to`\n\r\nRelationships may be simple edges or first-class nodes when the relationship itself needs evidence, provenance, dispute, or revision history.\r\n\r\nRelationship records should preserve assertion provenance when available. Common fields include `asserted_by`, `asserted_at`, `confidence`, `evidence`, and `tumbler`.\n\n`asserted_by` should point to a person, organization, project, or external actor identifier when possible. If the actor is local to the substrate, it should be represented as a node.\n\nRelationships may also carry temporal validity when the relationship was true during a specific historical interval. Use `valid_from` for the beginning of the interval and `valid_to` for the end. These fields describe the modeled reality, while `asserted_at` describes when the substrate recorded the assertion.\n\n## Projection Style\n\nRelationship type registries define canonical projection style metadata:\n\n- `color`\n- `inverse_color`\n- `line_style`\n- `inverse_line_style`\n- optional `projection` media metadata\n\nProjection layers use these values for connective lines, arrows, trails, legends, and other relationship marks. This is not decoration only: visual stratification helps readers distinguish evidence, lineage, authorship, governance, uncertainty, communication, and other relationship families before reading every label.\n\nA protocol-compliant projection may adapt contrast for accessibility, theme, or media constraints, but it must preserve the registry-level distinction between relationship types and their inverse readings. If a renderer cannot display color, it should use line style, labels, or another accessible cue instead of flattening every relationship into the same visual treatment.\n\nRelationship type registries may also declare projection media:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"type\": \"supports\",\n  \"category\": \"evidence\",\n  \"projection\": {\n    \"icon\": \"supports\",\n    \"icon_label\": \"->\",\n    \"asset_path\": \"assets/projection/relationship-types/supports.svg\",\n    \"category_asset_path\": \"assets/projection/relationship-categories/evidence.svg\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nRelationship projection assets should be carried as `media` nodes when bundled in a substrate or pack. Use `asset_role: \"relationship_type_projection_icon\"` for a specific relationship type and `asset_role: \"relationship_category_projection_icon\"` for category-level assets. Projection layers may use these assets in legends, path explainers, relationship catalogs, tooltips, and edge adornments, while edge color and line style still come from the relationship registry.\n\n## Inquiry, Uncertainty, And Workflow\n\nXanaNode relationships should preserve knowledge states, not only settled assertions.\n\nUse inquiry relationships when a node participates in research or explanation:\n\n- `answers`\n- `partially_answers`\n- `fails_to_answer`\n- `raises`\n- `investigates`\n- `motivates_inquiry`\n- `tests`\n- `falsifies`\n- `validates`\n\nUse workflow and uncertainty relationships when a node needs additional work:\n\n- `requires_information`\n- `requires_source`\n- `requires_review`\n- `requires_validation`\n- `requires_context`\n- `insufficient_evidence`\n- `unresolved`\n- `under_review`\n- `speculative`\n\nThese relationships make gaps explicit. A substrate should be able to say \"this claim needs a source\" or \"this question remains unresolved\" without pretending the missing knowledge is already known.\n\n## Discovery And Cognitive Relationships\n\nNot every useful connection is strong enough to be evidence, influence, or identity. Early research often begins with low-commitment associations.\n\nUse discovery and cognitive relationships for provisional connective tissue:\n\n- `possibly_related_to`\n- `shares_pattern_with`\n- `analogous_to`\n- `resonates_with`\n- `generalizes`\n- `specializes`\n- `abstraction_of`\n- `example_of`\n\nAuthors should prefer more specific high-commitment relationships when the evidence supports them. Low-commitment relationships are still valuable because they preserve the path by which a stronger interpretation may later be discovered.\n\n## Communication Relationships\n\nCommunication has structure. When the communication itself matters, represent it as a `communication` node and connect participants, questions, responses, audiences, and consequences with typed relationships.\n\nCanonical communication relationships include:\n\n- `asked`\n- `asked_in`\n- `answers`\n- `answered_in`\n- `replied_to`\n- `broadcast_to`\n- `communicated_to`\n- `presented_to`\n- `discussed_with`\n- `debated_with`\n- `negotiated_with`\n- `announced`\n- `reported_to`\n\nThis lets a substrate model knowledge flow: who asked, who answered, where the exchange happened, how it reached an audience, and what claims, projects, or gaps emerged from it.\n\n## Inverse Views\n\nCanonical relationship types are authored in one direction only. A type may name an `inverse` label for display or query purposes, but that inverse does not need to be registered as a second canonical type.\n\nFor example, an implementation can display `created_by` when viewing the target of a `created` relationship, but the stored relationship type remains `created`. This avoids bloating the canonical registry and avoids dangling inverse-type pairs.\n",

      "content": "# XanaNode Studio Guidance\n\nXanaNode Studio is an editor concept for local-first knowledge substrate authoring.\n\nIt should be understood as:\n\n> VS Code for knowledge substrates, hiding Git until the user is ready.\n\n## Principles\n\n- Do not reinvent Git.\n- Use Git as the substrate versioning layer.\n- Present Git concepts in human language first.\n- Keep the published substrate preview close to the real Hugo/static output.\n- Make validation actionable rather than cryptic.\n\n## Git UX Translation\n\n| Git concept | Studio language |\n|---|---|\n| commit | save snapshot |\n| branch | draft path |\n| merge | bring changes together |\n| pull request | propose changes |\n| diff | what changed |\n| log | history |\n\nPower users may reveal the Git layer directly.\n\n## Core Workspace\n\nStudio should use a three-panel model:\n\n- catalog and tools\n- live substrate view\n- node editor\n\nThe catalog is not a file tree. It should provide views by node type, trail, source, author, status, confidence, relationship cluster, recent edits, validation issues, unlinked nodes, and contradicted claims.\n\n## MVP\n\nThe first useful Studio should support:\n\n- create or open a substrate repo\n- show a node catalog\n- edit node content and metadata\n- add relationships with autocomplete\n- run validation\n- preview the Hugo implementation\n- save snapshots using Git\n- publish to GitHub Pages\n\n## Assistants\n\nFuture Studio assistants may include:\n\n- friendly validation repair\n- relationship suggestion\n- fragment maker\n- trail builder\n- source importer\n- media importer\n- review workflow\n- conflict UI\n- extension marketplace\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/studio.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:9e041388f1da9c6b3c40048f3850d7bb61482f4939b25681e2fadc7f05aa8df6",

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-substrates.md",
      "title": "Specs / Substrates",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "specs/substrates.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/substrates.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/substrates.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Substrates\n\nA substrate is an independently authored XanaNode-compatible knowledge graph.\n\nA substrate includes:\n\n- a manifest\n- a declared namespace\n- a Git repository backing the substrate history\n- nodes\n- relationships\n- schema declarations\n- optional custom extensions\n- optional merge reports\n- optional mounted or imported substrate layers\n\n## Standard Layout\n\nA substrate root is the directory containing `substrate.json`. A hand-built substrate should be usable from that root without private tool state.\n\nThe machine-readable layout registry is:\n\n- `schemas/substrate-layout.schema.json`\n- `schemas/xananode-substrate-layout.v0.1.0.json`\n\nThe minimal required file is:\n\n```text\nsubstrate.json\n```\n\nThe standard production layout is:\n\n```text\nsubstrate.json\nauthor-profile.json\nrelationships.json\nnodes/\n  *.json\nnodes.json\ncontent/\n  nodes/\n    *.md\nassets/\n  media/\n  sources/\n  projection/\nschemas/\n  *.json\nreports/\n  *.json\npacks/\n  <mounted-substrate>/\nimports/\n  <incoming-material>/\nro-crate-metadata.json\n.xananode/\n  workspace.json\n  authors.json\n```\n\nProtocol JSON (`nodes/*.json`, `nodes.json`, and `relationships.json`) is the interchange surface. Markdown under `content/nodes/` is an authoring projection used by current tools. A substrate can be authored directly as protocol JSON and still be valid.\n\nTools should preserve recognized files even when they do not understand every extension. Unknown local files under `assets/`, `schemas/`, `reports/`, `packs/`, or `imports/` are part of the substrate unless sharing or export policy explicitly excludes them.\n\n## Git Backing\n\nEach production substrate is a Git repository. Git provides the substrate's version history, branching, review, merge, and synchronization layer.\n\nXanaNode does not replace Git. It defines knowledge-specific artifacts inside a repository:\n\n- `substrate.json`\n- `nodes-index.json`\n- `relationships.json`\n- `nodes/*.json`\n- optional `schemas/*.json`\n- optional merge and compatibility reports\n\nFor static-host discovery, a substrate should also expose a machine front door such as `/.well-known/xananode`, plus ordinary crawl hints like `robots.txt` and head links that point to the protocol artifacts. The human viewer may live at the root, but the machine handshake should make the substrate obvious without scraping page prose or client-side graph code.\n\nThe substrate manifest must include a `repository` block describing the Git remote and default branch. Example fixtures may live in subdirectories of the protocol repository, but independently maintained substrates should use the repository root as the substrate root.\n\nSubstrates are sovereign. They can be moderated independently while still participating in federation.\n\n## Portable Substrates And Composition\n\nIn current XanaNode language, the substrate is the primary unit. A `.substrate` file is a portable bundled substrate for transmission or release. This specification still uses `pack` in a few field names and compatibility notes because older tools shipped that wording first, but official user-facing tools should teach substrate, `.substrate`, mount, import, merge, and Intertwingle.\nA portable substrate is a set of XanaNode protocol artifacts that can be added to another substrate without losing the ownership boundary of the source substrate.\n\nPortable substrates use the same artifact shapes as a normal substrate:\n\n- optional `substrate.json`\n- `nodes/*.json` or any JSON file containing one node, an array of nodes, or `{ \"nodes\": [...] }`\n- `relationships.json` or any JSON file containing `{ \"relationships\": [...] }`\n- optional `assets/` files referenced by media node `asset_path` fields\n- optional schemas, reports, and companion metadata\n\nPortable substrates may be used in three composition modes:\n\n- `mounted`: the incoming substrate is included at build or analysis time, but remains externally governed. Its records are not copied into the receiving substrate as canonical local content.\n- `imported`: the incoming substrate's records are copied into generated local artifacts with provenance, but identity is not reconciled beyond explicit namespace mappings. Imported records must retain `imported_from`, substrate identity, and composition mode metadata.\n- `merged`: the incoming substrate is reconciled with the receiving substrate through identity mapping, duplicate detection, conflict handling, and review policy. Merged substrates should produce a merge or intake report.\n\nMounted substrates are the right model for optional example layers, lineage overlays, alternate interpretations, and domain extensions. Imported substrates are the right model when a substrate owner wants local generated artifacts for an external substrate without claiming authorship. Merged substrates are the right model when a substrate owner decides that incoming records should be reconciled with local canonical authorship.\n\n`absorbed` is a legacy alias for `imported`. New manifests should prefer `imported` or `merged` so tools can distinguish copying from identity reconciliation.\n\nThe substrate manifest may declare substrate references in `imports`:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"imports\": [\n    \"xananode:core\",\n    {\n      \"id\": \"example.minimal\",\n      \"source\": \"vendor/xananode-core/vendor/xananode-protocol/examples/minimal-substrate\",\n      \"mode\": \"mounted\",\n      \"version\": \"0.2.0\",\n      \"required\": true\n    }\n  ]\n}\n```\n\nString imports remain valid for simple vocabulary or schema dependencies. Object imports are used when the import is a concrete substrate source.\n\nThe field name is `imports` for manifest compatibility, but user interfaces may describe these records as mounted substrates, federation sources, or substrate mounts. The important distinction is governance: a mounted substrate remains someone else's authored source unless an explicit import or merge step changes that status.\n\nSubstrate ingress may declare namespace mappings:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"id\": \"xananode.lineage\",\n  \"source\": \"imports/lineage\",\n  \"mode\": \"mounted\",\n  \"namespace_mappings\": [\n    {\n      \"from\": \"xananode.example\",\n      \"to\": \"xananode.com\",\n      \"scope\": \"relationships\",\n      \"reason\": \"Bind example-authored relationship endpoints to the receiving canonical substrate namespace.\"\n    }\n  ]\n}\n```\n\nNamespace mappings are local federation rules. They do not rename the imported substrate's own nodes unless `scope` is `all`; with `scope: \"relationships\"` they only rebase relationship endpoints so mounted records can connect to equivalent local substrate nodes. Implementations should report mappings in review or merge/intake output so authors can audit how a mounted substrate is being interpreted.\n\nImplementations must not silently treat a mounted substrate as imported or merged. If a tool copies mounted records into local artifacts, it should record that as an explicit import step with review metadata. If a tool reconciles identity, de-duplicates nodes, or resolves conflicting claims, it should record that as a merge step. This prevents two repositories from accidentally claiming canonical ownership of the same governed records.\n\nWhen a substrate is cloned from an online federation target, the mounted name must include the Git branch and commit that were actually used, such as `Example Substrate (main@1a2b3c4d5e6f)`. Branch plus commit is the concrete version. A branch name alone is not enough provenance because the branch can move.\n\n## Substrate Media Portability\n\nPortable substrates that include local media must include the media files alongside the node records. Media files belong under the substrate root, normally in `assets/`. Media nodes reference those files with relative `asset_path` values.\n\nSubstrate builders must:\n\n- copy every file referenced by a media node `asset_path`\n- copy authored local substrate files that the substrate carries as evidence or media, including images, PDFs, audio, video, diagrams, source snapshots, and other digital files unless sharing policy excludes them\n- reject or warn on paths that escape the substrate root\n- preserve `source_url`, `rights_status`, `license`, `source_snapshot`, and content identifiers when known\n- keep `primary_media` references pointing at media node ids, not raw file paths\n- include projection media assets referenced by node type, subtype, relationship type, and relationship category registries when the substrate claims to carry those registries\n- report duplicate local files by content hash so authors can see when the same literal file has propagated through multiple substrates\n- report path conflicts when two different files claim the same relative `asset_path`\n\nSubstrate consumers must resolve `asset_path` relative to the source substrate root for mounted substrates and relative to the receiving substrate root for imported or merged assets after an explicit copy step.\n\nLive source URLs and captured media are different objects. A `source` node may point to `https://example.com`. A related `media` node with `subtype: \"web_snapshot\"` may carry a screenshot or Open Graph image captured from that URL. This lets renderers show rich previews while preserving source identity, capture time, rights, and provenance.\n\nWhen two mounted substrates carry the same literal file, tools should preserve provenance from both substrates instead of blindly copying both files forever. Permanent federation or merge may keep one canonical media node, record duplicate-file evidence in the merge report, and use transclusion or media relationships from the other substrate back to the retained file. That keeps the file lineage visible without multiplying identical bytes.\n\nLinking to an online file is allowed, but a portable substrate should prefer a local captured asset or a reference to a media node in another mounted substrate when the file matters to the knowledge claim. Online links rot; substrate-carried media preserves the evidence path.\n\n## Substrate Archives\n\nA portable substrate may be transmitted as a single `.substrate` archive. The archive is the whole substrate unit: manifest, nodes, relationships, media, registry extensions, source snapshots, and workspace metadata needed to keep the files together.\n\nThe recommended media type is `application/vnd.xananode.substrate+json+gzip`. The archive should contain a JSON envelope compressed with gzip. Tools may support other containers later, but official XanaNode tooling should read and write this format.\n\nMounted substrate records should preserve:\n\n- repository URL\n- branch\n- commit hash\n- archive file name when exported\n- namespace\n- mounted/imported/merged/absorbed mode\n\n## Executable Tool Contract\n\nThe protocol does not require Node.js, Python, or any other language runtime. Those are implementation choices, not protocol rules.\n\nOfficial XanaNode tooling should converge on two boring machine entry points that other applications can call regardless of stack:\n\n- `xananode-core`\n- `xananode-workspace`\n\nThese may be delivered as native executables, self-contained packaged binaries, platform launchers, or language-specific wrappers, but the command contract should remain stable across forms.\n\nThe intent is simple:\n\n- Core handles protocol reading, validation, analysis, fragment generation, bundle writing, and projection-ready graph derivation.\n- Workspace handles substrate folder lifecycle, authoring roots, imports, working copies, federation intake, archive packaging, and build orchestration.\n\nApplications such as Studio, Mobile, Hugo helpers, AI agents, browser services, and third-party integrations should be able to call those tools without embedding the implementation language directly.\n\n### Required executable behavior\n\nOfficial executable implementations should:\n\n- accept ordinary filesystem paths to substrate folders, `.substrate` archives, `substrate-bundle.json`, and `substrate-bundle.jsonl`\n- return structured machine-readable output through JSON on stdout, JSON files on disk, or both\n- return non-zero exit codes on validation, parse, or runtime failure\n- preserve protocol semantics regardless of host language\n- expose build metadata such as version, commit, build date, and implementation identity\n- keep command names and output envelopes stable enough that one tool can replace another implementation without rewriting the whole caller\n\n### Recommended Core commands\n\nThe stable Core surface should include commands equivalent to:\n\n- `xananode-core validate`\n- `xananode-core inspect`\n- `xananode-core build`\n- `xananode-core bundle`\n- `xananode-core analyze-intake`\n- `xananode-core projection`\n\nExpected responsibilities:\n\n- `validate`: schema and integrity validation without mutating source records\n- `inspect`: summarize a substrate or portable substrate source\n- `build`: emit split protocol artifacts and optional bundle variants\n- `bundle`: emit a portable `.substrate` archive plus optional JSON and JSONL companions\n- `analyze-intake`: evaluate an incoming substrate against a receiving substrate for merge candidates, conflicts, suggested links, suggested transclusions, duplicate files, and health signals\n- `projection`: derive renderer-neutral graph/projection data from protocol artifacts\n\n### Recommended Workspace commands\n\nThe stable Workspace surface should include commands equivalent to:\n\n- `xananode-workspace init`\n- `xananode-workspace open`\n- `xananode-workspace build`\n- `xananode-workspace export`\n- `xananode-workspace intertwingle`\n- `xananode-workspace deintertwingle`\n- `xananode-workspace health`\n- `xananode-workspace snapshot`\n\nExpected responsibilities:\n\n- `init`: create a compliant substrate folder with declared authorship and repository metadata\n- `open`: open an existing local substrate folder or working copy\n- `build`: orchestrate Core builds for the active workspace\n- `export`: write `.substrate`, `substrate-bundle.json`, `substrate-bundle.jsonl`, and split artifact outputs from the same source substrate\n- `intertwingle`: mount or clone another substrate into a local workspace without silently claiming ownership\n- `deintertwingle`: remove mounted material that has not been explicitly merged, while warning about broken chains or lost references\n- `health`: compute substrate health, intake reports, chain break warnings, summary/body duplication warnings, and other authoring signals\n- `snapshot`: create a versioned workspace snapshot through the substrate's backing VCS\n\n### JSON-first machine I/O\n\nFor interoperability, executable implementations should support JSON-first invocation patterns.\n\nExamples:\n\n- pass arguments by filesystem path and flags\n- optionally accept JSON request files or stdin envelopes\n- write JSON result envelopes to stdout when `--json` is requested\n\nThe exact envelope format may evolve, but official tools should preserve these principles:\n\n- callers should not scrape prose output\n- success and failure should be obvious without reading human narration\n- structured warnings should be distinguishable from fatal errors\n- file outputs should be declared in the response\n\n### Relationship to language-specific packages\n\nJavaScript, Python, Rust, Go, Java, Kotlin, or other language packages may still exist as implementation surfaces. That is fine.\n\nBut the protocol-facing contract should be the executable behavior and the artifact formats, not a requirement that every downstream caller embed Node.js APIs directly.\n\nIn other words: the substrate is the protocol object, and the executable contract is the boring transport layer that lets many tools participate without sharing one language runtime.\n\nThe current reference stack now supports Windows launcher packaging for `xananode-core` and `xananode-workspace` as self-contained executables with sibling runtime trees. That packaging shape is still an implementation detail, but it proves the protocol boundary can be used without a separate Node install on the calling machine.\n\n## Federation Targets\n\nThe protocol registry may list known online federation targets. A federation target is a Git-backed substrate source that tools can discover, clone, validate, and mount into a local workspace.\n\nThe registry is a discovery aid, not a central authority. A substrate can federate with targets outside the registry as long as their manifests, schemas, and sharing rules validate.\n\nWorkspace-level tools are responsible for cloning, naming, caching, mounting, and exporting mixed substrates. Studio may expose those actions, but the substrate management behavior belongs below the projection layer.\n\n## Sharing Policy\n\nNodes are shareable by default. This matches the federation model: an authored substrate is expected to be portable unless an author marks specific records as private, restricted, or needing review.\n\nA substrate may declare a top-level `sharing` policy:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"sharing\": {\n    \"default_shareable\": true,\n    \"excluded_nodes\": [\"personal:person/private-contact\"],\n    \"excluded_trails\": [\"personal:trail/private-family-history\"],\n    \"rules\": [\n      {\n        \"selector\": { \"tag\": \"medical\" },\n        \"shareable\": false,\n        \"scope\": \"private\",\n        \"reason\": \"Contains sensitive personal context.\"\n      }\n    ]\n  }\n}\n```\n\nIndividual nodes may override that default:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"id\": \"personal:claim/something-i-can-share\",\n  \"sharing\": {\n    \"shareable\": true,\n    \"scope\": \"public\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nWhen a trail is excluded, tools should treat the trail node and the trail's listed members as excluded unless an individual node explicitly opts back in. When a relationship touches a non-shareable node, pack exporters must omit that relationship or replace it with an explicit redaction record if the export profile supports redactions.\n\nCore, Workspace, Studio, and official projection layers must respect `sharing` during export, publication, federation, mounted-pack preparation, and generated review reports. A renderer may show private records inside a local authoring session, but it must not publish them into static public artifacts.\n\n## Projection Governance\n\nProjection layers are lenses over a substrate. They may choose layout, typography, motion, interaction, and density, but official XanaNode projections must preserve protocol semantics:\n\n- relationship direction must be visible when edges are shown\n- relationship colors and line styles should come from the relationship type registry, with accessible alternatives when color alone is insufficient\n- node titles should remain titles; official graph projections should use node type/subtype projection media or primary media as the node center mark\n- node type, subtype, and multi-type/facet signals should remain distinguishable through projection media, color mixing, subtle chips, legends, tooltips, or inspector metadata\n- hop-depth controls should actually filter by graph distance from the focused node\n- when a reader travels along visible connected nodes, the projection should animate the walked route rather than dragging a decorative line\n- when a reader jumps to an unconnected or hidden node, the projection should make that leap legible with staged reveal by hop depth\n\nCustom renderers can be compatible without copying the official look, but they should not flatten away direction, authorship, provenance, or sharing policy.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/substrates.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:bd7d3ad22ad8554a7033d1a123af3e22488c63d922e1c96de26e518875c9c720",

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-transclusion.md",
      "title": "Specs / Transclusion",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "specs/transclusion.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/transclusion.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/transclusion.md",
      "media_type": "document",

      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",

      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Transclusion\r\n\r\nTransclusion is inclusion by reference rather than duplication.\r\n\r\nIn XanaNode, transclusion should preserve:\r\n\r\n- source identity\r\n- attribution\r\n- version lineage\r\n- fragment address\r\n- rights metadata\r\n\r\nThe relationship type `transcludes` describes transclusion at the graph level. A viewer may derive the inverse view `transcluded_by` when looking from the fragment back to the consuming node.\n\r\nThe minimal interoperable form is:\r\n\r\n- a source node, such as `source/as-we-may-think`\r\n- a fragment node with `source_node`, `source_version_id`, `source_content_id`, `fragment_id`, `content_id`, `version_id`, `tumbler`, and `selector`\n- a consuming node, such as an essay or trail\r\n- a `transcludes` relationship from the consuming node to the fragment node\r\n\r\nThe `transcludes` relationship should carry the same versioned fragment `tumbler` used by the fragment node. This makes the transclusion point to a specific source version and fragment version rather than to a floating latest passage.\n\nImplementations may begin with practical fragment references before attempting full Project Xanadu-style transclusion.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/transclusion.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:b35e2c6a656a9f86ab4eb33096d1fce982a9ec24af53d9723da7d8db56dd523c",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/transclusion.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:b35e2c6a656a9f86ab4eb33096d1fce982a9ec24af53d9723da7d8db56dd523c",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-tumbler-addressing.md",
      "title": "Specs / Tumbler Addressing",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "specs/tumbler-addressing.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/tumbler-addressing.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/tumbler-addressing.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Tumbler Addressing\r\n\r\nTumbler addressing refers to persistent, location-independent addressing of knowledge objects and fragments.\r\n\r\nThe goal is to address a thing even if:\r\n\r\n- files move\r\n- folders change\r\n- URLs change\r\n- renderers change\r\n- the substrate is mirrored or federated\r\n\r\nXanaNode begins with stable node IDs and a minimal fragment addressing profile.\r\n\r\n## Address Profile\r\n\r\nNode addresses use:\r\n\r\n```text\r\n<namespace>:<node-kind>/<local-id>\r\n```\r\n\r\nVersioned node addresses add an immutable version identity:\n\n```text\n<namespace>:<node-kind>/<local-id>@<version-id>\n```\n\nFragment addresses append a fragment selector and must preserve both the source node version and the fragment version:\n\n```text\n<namespace>:<node-kind>/<local-id>@<source-version-id>#fragment/<fragment-id>@<fragment-version-id>\n```\n\r\nRules:\r\n\r\n- `namespace` is a registered namespace such as `xananode` or `example.minimal`.\r\n- `node-kind` is the node type family used in the substrate path, such as `source`, `claim`, `concept`, `essay`, or `fragment`.\r\n- `local-id` is stable within the namespace.\n- `source-version-id` identifies the exact source node version being addressed, such as a Git commit/path identity or immutable content ID.\n- `fragment-id` is stable within the addressed node.\n- `fragment-version-id` identifies the exact fragment extraction/version, usually a content hash or generated fragment revision ID.\n- Fragment nodes must store `source_node`, `source_version_id`, `source_content_id`, `fragment_id`, `content_id`, `version_id`, and `tumbler`.\n- A `tumbler` field must preserve the full versioned node or fragment address when a record depends on persistent addressing.\n\r\nExamples:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nexample.minimal:concept/knowledge-substrate\nexample.minimal:source/as-we-may-think@git:8e47e70:examples/minimal-substrate/nodes/source-as-we-may-think.json#fragment/0004@sha256:example-as-we-may-think-fragment-0004\n```\n\nUnversioned node addresses are stable identities. Versioned node and fragment tumblers are fixed references to an exact historical chunk. A transclusion should use the versioned fragment tumbler, not the floating node identity.\n\nThis profile is intentionally small. Future versions may add richer selectors, byte ranges, media regions, and additional revision systems without invalidating these base addresses.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/tumbler-addressing.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:5b621f0f1c8861b7a852efb7d128070bc8b8569a6325e7a067a1b90854c6bd65",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/tumbler-addressing.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:5b621f0f1c8861b7a852efb7d128070bc8b8569a6325e7a067a1b90854c6bd65",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-xananode-core.md",
      "title": "Specs / Xananode Core",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "specs/xananode-core.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/xananode-core.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/xananode-core.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# XanaNode Core\r\n\r\nXanaNode is an open protocol for interoperable knowledge substrates.\r\n\r\nThe core specification defines the shared vocabulary, object model, and protocol-level assumptions used across the rest of the repository.\r\n\r\n## Scope\r\n\r\n- the canonical node registry in [schemas/xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json](../schemas/xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json)\r\n- the canonical relationship registry in [schemas/xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json](../schemas/xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json)\n- the versioned JSON Schemas that validate those registries\r\n\r\n## Core Model\r\n\r\nThe current node registry centers on person, concept, claim, source, essay, and observation.\r\n\r\nThe current relationship registry centers on definitions, evidence, claim structure, lineage, identity, creation, participation, location, revision, and related semantic links.\r\n\r\nThe core model is designed to preserve provenance and disagreement rather than flattening distinct knowledge states into one asserted fact.\r\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/xananode-core.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:dde21bd72733f48e0092eaccad02e9debdf7cb0ebdcbed436cb2367f5e53e23b",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/xananode-core.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:dde21bd72733f48e0092eaccad02e9debdf7cb0ebdcbed436cb2367f5e53e23b",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },

      "content": "# Tools\r\n\r\nThis directory contains repository maintenance tools.\r\n\r\n## Validator\r\n\r\nRun:\r\n\r\n```bash\r\npython -m pip install -r requirements-dev.txt\r\npython tools/validate.py\r\n```\r\n\r\nThe validator checks JSON Schema conformance for protocol artifacts and XanaNode-specific integrity rules such as declared relationship types, registered namespaces, and resolvable relationship endpoints.\r\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/tools/README.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:0a0d8a1968acc33cae0944e7b1f26f18e97615bfef65fd1c2c6282947f6fcb7b",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/tools/README.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:0a0d8a1968acc33cae0944e7b1f26f18e97615bfef65fd1c2c6282947f6fcb7b",
        "rights_status": "Apache-2.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }

      "title": "XanaNode Trademark Policy",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 3,
      "summary": "TRADEMARK.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.",

      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# XanaNode Trademark Policy\n\nThe XanaNode name, XanaNode logo, and related brand assets identify the canonical XanaNode protocol and project.\n\nYou may use the name XanaNode to make accurate factual statements, such as:\n\n- \"implements the XanaNode protocol\"\n- \"XanaNode-compatible\"\n- \"built with XanaNode schemas\"\n- \"uses XanaNode Core\"\n\nYou may not use the XanaNode name or logo in a way that implies endorsement, official status, or control by the XanaNode project unless that relationship is true.\n\nImplementations should identify themselves as independently authored unless they are maintained by the XanaNode project. For example:\n\n> ExampleApp is an independent XanaNode-compatible implementation.\n\nModified logos, confusingly similar marks, and product names that imply ownership of XanaNode itself are not permitted without permission.\n\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/TRADEMARK.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:920cc1e4e4719f3383ec0c50e4eafba89f848b730b4d297b3a22ab576edde81c",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-25T14:22:39.906Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/TRADEMARK.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:920cc1e4e4719f3383ec0c50e4eafba89f848b730b4d297b3a22ab576edde81c",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft",
      "title": "XanaNode As A Kernel For Human Thought Draft",
      "type": "source",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A raw authored comparison that frames XanaNode as an open foundational substrate layer for knowledge in the way Linux became an open foundational kernel for computing.",
      "created_by": "xananode.canonical:person/christian-siefen",
      "created_at": "2026-06-22T00:00:00.000Z",
      "rights_status": "canonical-public",
      "content_id": "sha256:xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft-source",
      "version_id": "sha256:xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft-source-v1",
      "content": "When Linus Torvalds created Linux, he did not build a flashy consumer application. He built an open foundational kernel, a plumbing layer that handles how a computer operates, manages memory, and talks to hardware. Because that plumbing was open, standard, and free, it became the invisible engine underneath Android phones, enterprise cloud servers, smart TVs, and supercomputers.\n\nXanaNode is trying to do the same thing for knowledge. It is not a notes app trying to trap attention inside one interface. It is a substrate protocol and stack layer meant to let knowledge move between tools without losing its structure.\n\nThe Core SDK plays the role of shared plumbing. It is renderer-independent and handles the baseline rules of structure, relationship typing, fragment tracking, validation, and export so every projection does not have to reinvent them.\n\nThe substrate and the projection are intentionally separated. In Linux, people interact through distributions such as Ubuntu, Fedora, or Red Hat while the kernel remains the underlying contract. In XanaNode, the same substrate can be viewed through Studio, Mobile, Hugo, or a future projection that does not exist yet. The tool is a lens over the data, not the data itself.\n\nThat separation matters because it prevents knowledge from being held hostage by one product. If a proprietary notes tool collapses, changes pricing, or drifts away from its users, the knowledge goes with it. XanaNode keeps the substrate local, protocol-shaped, and portable, so another compatible projection can pick it up and keep going.\n\nFederation is the ecosystem layer. Linux did not become important because one company owned all computation. It mattered because many independent actors could build on a common base. XanaNode aims for the same pattern with independently authored substrates that can mount, compare, and merge without surrendering provenance, lineage, disagreement, or ownership.\n\nThe point is not another app. The point is an open substrate ecosystem for durable human-to-machine knowledge transfer.",
      "relationships": []
    },

      "id": "xananode.canonical:trail/federated-stack-proof",
      "title": "Federated Stack Proof",
      "type": "trail",
      "subtype": "evidence_path",
      "importance": 5,
      "summary": "A short trail showing that the XanaNode stack proves federation by publishing its own layers as interoperable substrates.",
      "content": "Follow the stack from the protocol substrate into Core, then outward into Workspace, Studio, Mobile, and Hugo. The canonical substrate points to each real layer as a federated substrate source, while also carrying embedded example namespaces that teach the same federation patterns in a portable local bundle. Together these show that the stack is not one giant opaque application. It is a federation of layers that remain interoperable without surrendering identity.",
